recipes.title: Jollof Rice
recipes.description: Nigerian one-pot tomato-pepper rice, smoky and richly spiced, the centerpiece of every party.
steps: Blend tomatoes, bell peppers, scotch bonnet, and one onion until smooth.
Reduce the puree in a saucepan over medium heat for 15 minutes until thickened.
In a heavy pot, heat oil and sauté the second onion for 4 minutes.
Add tomato paste and fry for 5 minutes until darkened.
Pour in the reduced pepper mix, curry, thyme, bay leaves, and salt. Cook for 10 minutes.
Stir in the rinsed rice to coat in the sauce.
Add stock, cover tightly with foil and a lid. Cook on low for 25 minutes without stirring.
Increase heat for the last 3 minutes to develop the smoky bottom (party jollof).
Fluff and serve hot.

recipes.title: Egusi Soup
recipes.description: Nigerian thick soup of ground melon seeds, leafy greens, and assorted meats, eaten with swallow.
steps: Season and boil the assorted meat with onion and stock cubes for 30 minutes until tender.
Mix the ground egusi with a little water to form a paste.
Heat palm oil in a pot for 2 minutes (do not bleach).
Add chopped onion and the blended scotch bonnet, fry for 3 minutes.
Drop spoonfuls of the egusi paste into the oil and let them fry for 6 minutes without stirring, then break apart.
Pour in the meat and its stock plus enough water. Add crayfish and salt.
Simmer for 15 minutes, stirring occasionally.
Add smoked fish and chopped greens. Cook for another 5 minutes.
Serve with pounded yam, fufu, or eba.

recipes.title: Pounded Yam
recipes.description: Smooth, stretchy Nigerian yam swallow traditionally pounded with mortar and pestle to eat with soups.
steps: Place yam cubes in a pot with enough water to cover.
Add salt and bring to a boil.
Cook for 20-25 minutes until very soft when pierced with a knife.
Drain the yam, reserving a cup of cooking water.
Transfer hot yam to a mortar (or sturdy bowl).
Pound or beat with a wooden pestle, adding small splashes of cooking water, until smooth and stretchy.
Wet hands and shape into round mounds.
Serve immediately alongside egusi or ogbono soup.

recipes.title: Ogbono Soup
recipes.description: Nigerian draw soup made from ground wild mango seeds, with rich palm oil and assorted meats.
steps: Boil meat with onion, stock cubes, and salt for 30 minutes until tender.
Heat palm oil in a separate pot for 2 minutes.
Whisk the ground ogbono into the warm oil to form a smooth paste.
Slowly stir in the meat stock and water to loosen the soup.
Add the cooked meat, smoked fish, crayfish, and blended scotch bonnet.
Simmer covered for 15 minutes until thick and drawing.
Add the chopped spinach and cook for 4 minutes more.
Adjust salt and serve with fufu or eba.

recipes.title: Suya
recipes.description: Nigerian street-food beef skewers crusted in spicy peanut yaji rub and grilled over open flame.
steps: Slice beef thinly against the grain and pat dry.
Coat the beef in groundnut oil, then dredge thoroughly in the yaji rub and salt.
Thread onto soaked wooden skewers, leaving small gaps.
Let the skewers rest for 30 minutes.
Grill over hot charcoal for 3 minutes per side until edges char.
Sprinkle a final dusting of yaji over the cooked skewers.
Serve hot with raw onion, tomato, and cabbage on the side.

recipes.title: Akara
recipes.description: Crispy Nigerian fritters made of peeled black-eyed beans, onions, and chili, deep-fried golden.
steps: Soak the black-eyed beans in cold water for 1 hour.
Rub the beans between your hands to remove the skins, then rinse them away.
Blend the peeled beans with onion, scotch bonnet, bell pepper, and just enough water for a thick paste.
Whisk the batter vigorously for 5 minutes to incorporate air.
Stir in the salt right before frying.
Heat oil to 170°C and drop tablespoons of batter in.
Fry for 4-5 minutes, turning, until deeply golden and puffed.
Drain on paper towels and serve hot with bread or pap.

recipes.title: Moin Moin
recipes.description: Steamed Nigerian bean pudding of blended black-eyed peas, peppers, and palm oil, often with egg and fish.
steps: Soak beans in water for 1 hour, then peel them by rubbing.
Blend the peeled beans with peppers, scotch bonnet, and onion until very smooth.
Transfer to a bowl and whisk in palm oil, crayfish, stock cubes, and salt.
Stir in flaked smoked fish.
Pour into greased ramekins or banana leaf bowls, adding a slice of egg to each.
Arrange in a steamer with simmering water below.
Cover and steam for 50-60 minutes until firm and set.
Cool slightly before unmolding and serving with pap or bread.

recipes.title: Pepper Soup
recipes.description: Nigerian aromatic broth of catfish or goat meat with calabash nutmeg and uziza, intensely peppery.
steps: Rinse the catfish in salted hot water and drain (this firms the flesh).
Bring water to a boil with onion, ginger, garlic, and stock cubes.
Add the pepper soup spice mix and scotch bonnet. Simmer for 10 minutes.
Carefully add the catfish pieces.
Simmer gently for 12-15 minutes without stirring, just shaking the pot.
Adjust salt and add the uziza leaves in the last 2 minutes.
Serve hot in bowls with white rice or as a starter.

recipes.title: Efo Riro
recipes.description: Yoruba stew of sautéed leafy greens with peppers, locust beans, and assorted meats in palm oil.
steps: Blend the bell peppers and scotch bonnet to a coarse paste.
Heat palm oil in a pot and sauté onion for 4 minutes.
Pour in the pepper blend and fry for 10 minutes until oil floats on top.
Stir in iru, crayfish, and stock cubes. Cook for 3 minutes.
Add the cooked assorted meat and smoked fish, simmer for 8 minutes.
Fold in the chopped greens and cook for 5 minutes until just wilted.
Adjust salt and serve hot with pounded yam or rice.

recipes.title: Puff Puff
recipes.description: Sweet, soft Nigerian fried dough balls with a crisp golden exterior and pillowy center.
steps: In a bowl, whisk warm water, sugar, and yeast. Let bloom for 5 minutes.
Add flour, nutmeg, and salt. Whisk into a smooth, sticky batter.
Cover and let rise in a warm place for 1 hour until bubbly and doubled.
Heat oil to 170°C in a deep pot.
Scoop balls of batter using a wet hand or ice cream scoop and drop into the oil.
Fry in batches for 4-5 minutes, turning, until deep golden brown all around.
Drain on paper towels.
Dust with sugar and serve warm.

recipes.title: Chin Chin
recipes.description: Crunchy Nigerian fried dough snack cut into small cubes, lightly sweet and shelf-stable.
steps: In a bowl mix flour, sugar, baking powder, nutmeg, and salt.
Rub in the butter until the mixture resembles breadcrumbs.
Whisk milk and egg together and add to the dry ingredients.
Knead briefly into a stiff but pliable dough.
Roll out 5 mm thick on a floured surface and cut into small cubes.
Heat oil to 170°C and fry the cubes in batches for 4-5 minutes until deep golden.
Stir constantly so they brown evenly.
Drain and cool completely before storing in an airtight jar.

recipes.title: Ofada Stew
recipes.description: Yoruba ayamase stew of green bell peppers and locust beans cooked in bleached palm oil, served with ofada rice.
steps: Blend the green peppers, scotch bonnet, and half the onion to a coarse paste.
Reduce the pepper blend in a dry pot for 15 minutes until almost dry.
In a separate pot, bleach palm oil over medium heat for 8-10 minutes until light, then turn off and cool slightly.
Slice the remaining onion and fry in the bleached oil for 3 minutes.
Add iru, crayfish, and stock cubes. Stir for 2 minutes.
Pour in the reduced pepper paste and fry for 10 minutes.
Add the cooked meat and a little stock, simmer for 10 minutes.
Add boiled eggs and salt, simmer 3 more minutes. Serve over ofada rice.
